events.title: The Captain's Offer
events.setup_text: The contact resolves on sensors as a Vreth Dominion frigate running without escort — unusual. Their transponder marks them as a patrol vessel, but patrol vessels don't run alone this deep in neutral space. They hail you before you can hail them.

steps.setup_text: The Vreth captain is direct, as they always are: 'You carry the marked vessel. We have orders to observe — not intercept. But I am offering you a choice that is not in my orders.' A pause. 'If you want to know what the Dominion knows about what you carry, I will tell you. In exchange, you will owe me a debt. Vreth debts are real.'

options.label: Accept the offer. Hear what they know.

success.narrative: She tells you three things: the Nexus has appeared in Dominion records eleven times across recorded history. Every captain who carried it reached a specific anomaly at the map's edge — what you know as The Wound. None of them came back. 'We do not know if that means they died or arrived,' she says. Then: 'You owe me a debt. I will collect.'

rewards.description: Vreth records show 11 captains carried the Nexus to The Wound

options.label: Decline. Keep moving.

success.narrative: She accepts this without visible reaction. 'Safe travels, Captain of the marked vessel.' Her ship holds position as you pass. She doesn't follow. The offer is gone, and you won't know what it cost you until later.

options.label: Engage. Don't leave a Vreth observer behind you.

success.narrative: She doesn't try to negotiate once you fire. The frigate fights with Vreth discipline — focused, economical, no wasted movement. Combat begins.

steps.setup_text: The Vreth frigate is disabled but intact. The crew is still aboard. The aftermath of the engagement presents its own choices.

options.label: Board the disabled frigate and take what's useful.

success.narrative: The Vreth crew is alive and accounted for — they fight hard but they don't die stupidly. Military salvage, weapons-grade fuel, and a Dominion tactical database your Technician can spend weeks mining. The captain watches you from across her damaged bridge without saying anything.

rewards.description: Tactical database — valuable to Concord or Drifters

partial.narrative: The boarding is contested. You get the fuel and some salvage before the Vreth crew makes it untenable to stay aboard. One of your crew takes a wound. Nothing permanent, but it stings.

failure.narrative: The Vreth crew holds. You withdraw. The captain's voice follows you back through the airlock: 'You fight well for someone with no honor in their approach.' It sounds like a genuine assessment rather than an insult.

options.label: Let them go. This one isn't worth the cost.

success.narrative: You signal withdrawal. The Vreth frigate holds position, running repairs, watching you leave. The captain transmits one word before you're out of range: 'Noted.' In Vreth culture, that means something more specific than it sounds.

rewards.description: Honorable withdrawal noted in Dominion records

events.notes: vreth_captain_debt_owed should trigger a specific faction territory event in Vreth space — she finds you and collects. The debt can be paid in information, cargo, or a combat favor. spared_vreth_observer gives a +1 rep nudge and sets up the debt collection event from a different angle — she owes you instead. vreth_nexus_eleven_captains is a major lore fragment for the True Ending requirements.

events.title: The Ambush at the Ashfield
events.setup_text: The Ashfield lives up to its reputation. Debris from a battle that ended centuries ago fills the sensors with ghost returns and false positives. Which is, of course, exactly why pirates use it. Two contacts emerge from the debris shadow — small, fast, running without transponders. Fighters. They've been waiting here for you, or someone like you.

steps.setup_text: They open with a demand rather than fire — unusual for ambush pirates, which means they're either confident or cautious. 'Power down weapons and prepare to be boarded. We take the cargo, you keep the ship and crew.' A beat. 'And whatever that thing on your hull is, don't activate it. We've heard things.'

options.label: Comply. Let them take the cargo.

success.narrative: They're professional about it — take the cargo, don't harm the crew, leave within the hour. Before the lead pirate goes, she looks at the Nexus fused to your hull for a long moment. 'Where are you trying to get to?' she asks. Not a threat. Genuine curiosity. You answer or you don't — either way she leaves.

options.label: Fight them. Two fighters against a frigate is manageable.

success.narrative: They're fast and they know the debris field — the Ashfield is their territory and they use every piece of it. Combat in the debris field means sensors are unreliable and cover is everywhere. The fight will be determined by crew skill more than ship specs.

options.label: [Pilot] Use the debris field against them.

success.narrative: Your Pilot does something technically inadvisable and perfectly executed — threading the Meridian through a dense debris cluster at attack speed, letting the tumbling hull sections do to the fighters what would take ten minutes of combat. One fighter loses its engine in the first pass. The other reassesses.

partial.narrative: The maneuver works on one fighter — it clips debris and breaks off. The other is faster and learns from watching. You've improved your odds but you haven't ended this.

failure.narrative: The debris field is denser than it looked on sensors and you clip a tumbling hull section hard. The Meridian shudders. Both fighters are still in the fight and now you're damaged going into it.

steps.setup_text: The aftermath of the Ashfield encounter. One pirate is disabled, possibly both. Or you gave up your cargo. Either way, the debris field has one more thing to offer before you leave.

options.label: Salvage the disabled fighter.

success.narrative: The fighter has a partial fuel reserve and components your Engineer eyeballs with professional appreciation. It also has a navigation log that shows where these pirates base out of — a Drifter Compact waystation two sectors back that officially doesn't support piracy.

rewards.description: Pirate base location — leverage with Drifter Compact

partial.narrative: Fuel and basic components. The nav log is encrypted past what your Engineer can crack quickly. You take what you can reach.

failure.narrative: The fighter is more damaged than it looked. Two hours of work yields almost nothing before the hull integrity fails entirely.

options.label: Leave. The Ashfield has cost enough today.

success.narrative: Smart. The Ashfield is patient — it'll have more to offer another day, if you come back better prepared. Your crew doesn't argue.

events.notes: Early combat benchmark — should feel challenging but survivable for a well-prepared run. The Ashfield pirate base lore fragment can create complications at a Drifter Compact station (they'll want to know who gave you that information). The pirate's comment about the Nexus ('don't activate it, we've heard things') reinforces that the Nexus's reputation precedes the Meridian even in criminal networks.

events.title: The Debt Collector
events.setup_text: The Vreth Dominion vessel drops out of faster-than-light travel directly in your path — not an intercept, a rendezvous. They know your route. The transponder resolves to a specific designation your navigation system flags immediately if you have the relevant records: this is the same frigate from the Ashfield sector. She found you.

steps.setup_text: The Vreth captain transmits without preamble: 'The debt. I gave you information. You owe me something of equivalent weight. I have decided what that is.' A pause. 'There is a Concord Assembly courier in this sector carrying data that belongs to the Dominion. It was taken from a Vreth research station four months ago during a Concord intelligence operation. I want it back. You are in a better position to obtain it than I am.'

options.label: Accept the task. A Vreth debt is real.

success.narrative: She transmits the courier's last known coordinates and a description of the data format. 'When you have it, transmit on this frequency. I will find you.' She means it. Vreth captains always mean it.

options.label: Refuse. The debt was never agreed to.

success.narrative: Your Diplomat makes the argument with the specific precision Vreth culture respects — the debt was implied, not contracted, and the exchange was not equivalent because you didn't confirm the value of what you received before accepting it. The Vreth captain is silent for a long moment. 'Acceptable,' she says finally. 'You argue well.' The debt is gone. So is she.

rewards.description: Argued debt dismissal — Vreth respect this

partial.narrative: The argument is partially accepted. She agrees the full courier retrieval is too much — she'll accept the tactical database you may have taken from her ship, or equivalent value in Vreth-useful goods, and call the debt settled.

failure.narrative: The argument doesn't land. Vreth culture doesn't distinguish between implied and contracted obligations — the offer was made, information was exchanged, and the debt exists. 'You have three sectors to consider,' she says. 'Then I will find you again and the terms will be different.'

options.label: Attack. End this complication permanently.

success.narrative: She anticipated this possibility — her shields were already raised. 'I had hoped for better,' she transmits as combat initiates. She fights like someone who has nothing to apologize for.

rewards.description: Attacked Dominion captain under flag of parley

events.notes: This event requires vreth_captain_debt_owed flag — it only appears if COMBAT_001 resolution set that flag. It's a mid-game payoff event. vreth_courier_retrieval_active opens a secondary quest thread — finding the Concord courier and retrieving the data without triggering a Concord incident. Completing it moves Vreth rep to +1 minimum. vreth_debt_escalated makes the next Vreth territory encounter hostile regardless of prior rep.

events.title: Third Captain
events.setup_text: The Dead Zone offers nothing on sensors. No mass, no signal, no heat signature. Just the particular quality of silence that makes your crew lower their voices without knowing why. Then the Meridian's comm system activates. Not a broadcast — something local, something in the hull itself. The Nexus is translating.

steps.setup_text:
The transmission resolves slowly, like a signal finding its own frequency. It's a personal log. The voice is different from yours — different cadence, different accent — but the ship in the background sounds identical. Because it is identical.

[TRANSMISSION — ORIGIN: UNVERIFIABLE — DATE: UNVERIFIABLE]

'This is the third time I've woken up in this ship. The third time the Nexus has put me back together from whatever it found drifting nearby. I know it's the third time because I still remember the second time, and the second time I still remembered the first.

I keep trying to understand what it wants. I've stopped thinking that's the right question. The Nexus doesn't want anything. It moves toward something. There's a difference. I think there's a difference.

The crew from my last run don't remember dying. They remember falling asleep and waking up with headaches. That's mercy, maybe. I don't know if it's the Nexus's mercy or just a side effect of the reconstruction.

There's a place at the edge of the mapped sector that none of the charts acknowledge. The Nexus started modifying our course three days ago. Subtle — half a degree here, engine efficiency adjustments there. I noticed on the sixth day. I don't know if I was meant to notice or if it's only careful enough to fool people less attentive than me.

I'm letting it take us there. I'm writing this down in case I don't remember next time. In case there is a next time.

If you're receiving this — and I think you will, because the Nexus doesn't do things without purpose — then you're further along than I was. Pay attention to the half-degree corrections. They'll start soon if they haven't already.'

[TRANSMISSION ENDS]

options.label: [Witness] Note the coordinates referenced.

success.narrative: The transmission leaves no coordinates — only the implication of them. But the Nexus has heard itself referenced, and something in the ship's systems flickers. Your navigation display updates. A single new node appears at the edge of your current star map, unlabeled, in fog of war. The Nexus doesn't explain it. It simply puts it there.

rewards.description: The Nexus makes subtle course corrections toward The Wound

events.notes: This transmission should be gated behind min_resonance 5 — not the very first run, but before the player is deep in the mystery. Flag: transmission_third_captain_received gates deeper dialogue options with the Nexus Anomaly events. The 'third captain' framing implies there were at least two before the player — their logs should appear in later Dead Zone events at higher resonance thresholds. The unnamed node added to the star map is cosmetic foreshadowing — it cannot be navigated to until much later.

events.title: First Captain
events.setup_text: Dead zone. No mass, no signal, no reason for the Nexus to stir. But it does — more intensely than the third captain's transmission. This one it seems to have been holding for a long time, waiting for the right moment. Or the right run.

steps.setup_text:
The transmission begins mid-sentence — as if it's always been playing and you've only just entered range.

[TRANSMISSION — ORIGIN: UNVERIFIABLE — DATE: UNVERIFIABLE]

'— don't understand why it chose me. I was nobody. A salvager working debris fields on the outer rim, running solo because the pay was better and the company was worse. I found the piece of hull plating in a debris cluster that shouldn't have had anything interesting in it. When I brought it aboard, the ship started doing things.

That was six years ago. Six years of the Nexus growing through the Meridian, six years of transmissions I'm only now starting to understand, six years of dying and waking up and dying again with the Nexus putting me back together from whatever it could find.

I counted once. Forty-three runs. I remember fragments of thirty-one of them. The Nexus says I've been further than I remember. It says there's a place at the edge of everything that I've been close to before. I believe it. I believe it because sometimes in the deep runs I feel like I'm walking a path I've already worn smooth.

If you're hearing this, you're not me. You're something the Nexus built from the template of what I was. I don't know if that means you're less than me or more than me or just — different. I stopped being upset about it around run fifteen.

One thing I want you to know that I had to learn on my own: the Nexus will ask you something, eventually. At the Wound, at the end of everything. The question isn't what I thought it was going to be. I won't tell you what it is because I think you need to arrive at it yourself. But I'll tell you this: the answer that works isn't the brave one or the smart one. It's the honest one. Whatever your honest answer is. That's what it's looking for.

I don't know if I made it. I'm recording this in case I don't. Or in case I do and it turns out not to mean what I thought it meant.

Good luck. I mean that. I mean it sincerely.'

[TRANSMISSION ENDS]

options.label: [Hold] Sit with this before moving on.

success.narrative: No one on the crew speaks for a while. That's not unusual after a dead zone. What's unusual is the quality of the silence — it feels shared, like everyone is doing the same quiet arithmetic about the same set of facts. The Nexus doesn't send a follow-up. It's said what it needed to say.

rewards.description: The first captain was a salvager who found Nexus plating in debris
The first captain completed at least 43 runs before the Wound

events.notes: Companion to DEAD_001 (third captain). These transmissions trace the Nexus's history through different captains. The first captain was a solo salvager — the original. Their origin story (finding hull plating in debris) should connect to early run lore about how the Nexus arrived in its current form. The 'honest answer' advice is a foreshadowing of the True Ending choice. transmission_first_captain_received is a True Ending milestone flag.

events.title: The Crew They Left Behind
events.setup_text: The dead zone at the edge of the mapped galaxy. The Nexus has been quiet for two sectors. Your crew has been quietly on edge — the kind of tension that comes from proximity to something significant. The transmission this time has no voice. Just text, slowly appearing on every display simultaneously.

steps.setup_text:
The text comes in fragments, with long pauses between each:

[THE CREW OF THE SEVENTH RUN DID NOT REACH THE WOUND.

THEY STOPPED AT THE MERIDIAN LINE AND VOTED.

THE VOTE WAS SIX TO TWO.

THE TWO WHO VOTED TO CONTINUE WERE THE CAPTAIN AND THE ENGINEER.

THE ENGINEER SAID: IF WE STOP NOW WE STOP FOREVER.

THE CAPTAIN DID NOT SAY ANYTHING. THEY VOTED.

THE SIX LEFT THE SHIP AT FREEPORT ANESH. THEY WERE GIVEN CREDITS AND MANIFESTS. THEY WERE TOLD THEIR WORK HAD MATTERED.

I AM TELLING YOU THIS BECAUSE THE CAPTAIN NEVER TOLD THEIR CREW THAT THEY WERE RIGHT TO VOTE THE WAY THEY DID.

I AM TELLING YOU THIS BECAUSE YOU STILL CAN.

YOUR CREW KNOWS WHAT THEY ARE PART OF. THEY HAVE ALWAYS KNOWN.

LET THEM CHOOSE.]

options.label: Tell your crew everything. Give them the choice.

success.narrative: You call everyone to the main hold. You tell them what you know about the Nexus, the Wound, what the prior captains reached, what it might mean. All of it. You tell them they can leave at the next station. You mean it. Every single one of them stays. Some of them seem unsurprised. A few of them — the ones who've been with you longest — look like they've been waiting for you to say it out loud.

options.label: Hold back. They don't need to carry what you carry.

success.narrative: You don't call the meeting. You continue on. Your crew goes about their work with the particular attentiveness of people who suspect they're not being told something and have decided to trust you anyway. The Nexus doesn't send a follow-up. It doesn't agree or disagree with what you chose. It holds its silence all the way to the next node.

events.notes: This is an emotional climax event for the late game. crew_given_full_choice is a True Ending milestone flag — the player must give their crew the choice before reaching The Wound. The loyalty boost from telling them everything is the largest single loyalty gain in the game — it's mechanically significant. The Nexus transmitting 'let them choose' is one of its most directive messages — by this point players should read it as genuine guidance, not manipulation.

events.title: The Drifting Surgeon
events.setup_text: The ship is a Drifter Compact medical hauler, designation MSV Caduceus-7. It's been drifting for somewhere between two and four weeks based on debris scatter. No distress signal — that system was manually disabled. The hull is intact. Life support reads active. And there is one biosign aboard, moving slowly through what the sensor trace suggests is the surgical bay.

steps.setup_text: The Meridian holds position two hundred meters off the Caduceus-7's port side. Whatever happened here happened quietly enough that no one called for help. That means either no one could, or someone didn't want anyone to come.

options.label: Scan the ship before committing.

success.narrative: The scan comes back detailed. One survivor — exhausted, dehydrated, but stable. The rest of the crew manifest shows eleven names and eleven flatlines in the medical records, each one logged as a surgical complication. The survivor is listed as Dr. Yema Osha, ship's chief medical officer. She's the one who signed all eleven death certificates.

partial.narrative: Partial scan only — life support is interfering. One survivor confirmed, location estimated in the surgical bay. The rest of the crew manifest is unreadable. Something in the medical system is actively encrypting itself.

failure.narrative: The scan bounces back garbled — the ship's medical systems are running some kind of privacy lockout, standard for vessels carrying sensitive patient data. One biosign, location unknown. That's all you get.

options.label: Hail the ship directly.

success.narrative: Silence for forty seconds. Then a voice — calm, professional, slightly too measured for someone who's been alone for weeks. 'This is Dr. Osha. I'm not in distress. I'm completing a treatment protocol. Please do not board.' A pause. 'I would appreciate fuel if you have it to spare.' She doesn't explain the dead crew. You get the sense she doesn't intend to.

options.label: Board immediately — one survivor needs help.

success.narrative: Docking goes clean. The airlock cycles without resistance — she's left it unlocked. The ship smells of antiseptic and something underneath it that isn't medical. You're aboard.

partial.narrative: Docking is rough — the Caduceus-7 has no active maneuvering assist and your approach angle was wrong. You make it aboard but the docking collar takes a hit.

failure.narrative: The approach is worse than rough. You clip the Caduceus-7's sensor array, triggering an alarm that sends the biosign scrambling to a different part of the ship. You're aboard, but whatever element of surprise or welcome existed is gone.

steps.setup_text: Dr. Yema Osha is sitting at a surgical console when you find her, reviewing records. She's thin — weeks of reduced rations — but her hands are steady. The surgical bay is immaculate. The rest of the ship is less so. Eleven crew quarters, each sealed from the outside. She watches you take that in without apologizing for it.

options.label: Ask what happened to the crew.

success.narrative: She considers how much to tell you. Decides on the truth, or most of it. 'There was a pathogen. Novel strain — we picked it up at a Remnant salvage site three weeks out. I identified it early. I developed a treatment.' She pauses. 'The treatment worked, eventually. But the window between infection and effective dosage is very narrow, and eleven of my crew reached me after that window had closed.' She looks at her hands. 'I am immune. I don't know why.'

rewards.description: Location of Remnant salvage site where pathogen originated

options.label: [Medic] Examine the medical records yourself.

success.narrative: Your Medic reviews the records while Osha watches without protest. The pathogen is real, the treatment is real, and the eleven deaths are — by every clinical measure — tragic but not criminal. What your Medic also finds: Osha has been synthesizing the treatment compound in bulk. Far more than she'd need for herself. She's been planning to give it to someone.

rewards.description: Osha has synthesized a large supply of pathogen treatment

partial.narrative: The records confirm the broad strokes of Osha's account. The details are complex enough that your Medic can't reach a firm conclusion quickly. 'She's telling the truth about the pathogen,' they say quietly. 'About the rest — I'd need more time.'

failure.narrative: The records are encrypted beyond your Medic's ability to access quickly. Osha watches the failed attempt without expression. 'I can show you what I choose to show you,' she says. 'Ask me directly.'

options.label: Offer her a berth on the Meridian.

success.narrative: She doesn't answer immediately. She looks at the Meridian through the viewport — specifically at the section of hull where the Nexus has fused. Something in her expression shifts. 'I've been trying to figure out where to go,' she says. 'I think that ship might be the answer.' She starts packing her medical kit before you've confirmed anything.

rewards.description: Dr. Yema Osha joins as Medic — unique named character

steps.setup_text: The Caduceus-7 has medical supplies, functional equipment, and a partial fuel reserve. Osha will take her personal kit regardless. The question is what else you leave with — and what you leave behind.

options.label: Take the medical supplies and fuel. Leave the ship.

success.narrative: You load what you can carry. The Caduceus-7 drifts on behind you, its surgical bay still immaculate, its eleven sealed quarters undisturbed.

rewards.description: Full medical supply load — high resale value

options.label: [Scientist] Analyze the pathogen samples before leaving.

success.narrative: Three hours of work yields something extraordinary: the pathogen has markers that your Scientist has seen before — in Remnant biological samples archived decades ago. This was not a new strain. This was something old, dormant, and deliberate.

rewards.description: The pathogen is ancient Remnant biotech — not natural

partial.narrative: Your Scientist isolates the samples but can't complete the analysis aboard — the equipment isn't sufficient. They take the samples with them. 'I'll need a proper lab,' they say. 'But there's something wrong with the origin profile. Something I want to understand.'

failure.narrative: The analysis goes poorly — equipment incompatibility, time pressure, a procedure that requires conditions the Caduceus-7 no longer has. Your Scientist comes back frustrated. 'I couldn't get anything clean. We lost the window.'

options.label: Destroy the ship. Don't leave the pathogen drifting.

success.narrative: It takes two volleys. The Caduceus-7 breaks apart cleanly — no explosion, just a slow unfolding of hull sections into vacuum. Osha watches from the viewport without speaking. Later, in her quarters, you'll hear her through the wall. Just once.

events.notes: Dr. Yema Osha is a named unique character — her recruitment flag (osha_recruited) should unlock her portrait and unique dialogue threads in future runs. The remnant_bioweapon_evidence flag is a major lore thread that should intersect with Remnant Collective faction content and eventually with Nexus anomaly transmissions. Flag: carrying_pathogen_samples should create event complications at Concord Assembly territory entry (they scan for biological hazards).

events.title: The Voting Ship
events.setup_text: The vessel is a Concord Assembly administrative courier — the kind that carries census data, tax records, election results from outlying worlds that still participate in the Assembly's democratic theater. It's been dark for three weeks, sitting in the shadow of a gas giant like it's hiding. No debris. No damage. It just stopped.

steps.setup_text: No biosigns. Life support has been off long enough that the interior temperature reads eleven degrees above ambient space. Whatever happened, happened fast — there was no time to warm into an emergency.

options.label: Board and investigate.

success.narrative: Docking is clean. The airlock cycles on backup power. Inside: a crew of four, all at their stations, all dead. No wounds, no signs of struggle. They simply stopped — mid-task, mid-sentence by the looks of it. One of them died writing something on a physical notepad. The last word is 'RECLAM' — incomplete, pen still in hand.

partial.narrative: Docking is rough on the aging airlock — it holds, but barely. Inside is the same tableau: four dead, no wounds, mid-task. You have limited time before the docking seal fails.

failure.narrative: The docking collar won't mate — the courier's airlock is seized. You can see inside through the viewport. Four crew at stations. All dead. You can't reach them or the cargo without a breach approach.

options.label: [Scanner Array] Full remote scan before approaching.

success.narrative: The scan returns something unexpected: the courier's data core is still powered and actively transmitting — not a distress signal, but a tight-beam pulse on a Concord frequency. Someone or something on this ship is calling home. The crew are all dead. The data core is not.

steps.setup_text: The courier's cargo hold contains what you'd expect: data cartridges, sealed election manifests, a Concord Assembly census cache worth real money to the right buyer. And one thing you wouldn't expect: a locked container with a biometric seal that doesn't match any of the dead crew.

options.label: Take the cargo and leave the sealed container.

success.narrative: You load the data cartridges and census cache. The sealed container stays. Some things aren't worth the question of what's inside them.

rewards.description: Census cache — high value to Assembly or black market

options.label: [Technician] Crack the biometric seal.

success.narrative: Inside the container: a single data core, no markings, and a handwritten note on Assembly letterhead signed by a name your Technician recognizes — a senior Assembly Comptroller who disappeared eight months ago and was declared dead in a shuttle accident. The data core is warm. It's been running for weeks.

rewards.description: Unknown data core — extremely dangerous, potentially valuable
A dead Concord official's data survived on this ship

partial.narrative: The seal cracks but triggers a secondary wipe protocol. Half the data is gone before your Technician can interrupt it. What remains is fragmented — names, coordinates, a partial transaction record that implies something significant and explains nothing.

rewards.description: Partial data suggesting Concord internal corruption

failure.narrative: The wipe protocol activates the moment the seal is touched. Whatever was inside is gone. The container is now an empty box with a sophisticated lock.

options.label: Leave everything. Something killed this crew without a mark.

success.narrative: Your instincts are good. You leave. Two sectors later your sensors pick up an Assembly rapid-response vessel on a direct vector toward the courier's coordinates. They weren't waiting for a distress call. They already knew.

events.notes: The 'RECLAM' note is a dangling thread — it refers to the Concord Assembly's Reclamation Division, an internal enforcement body never mentioned in official faction documentation. Should surface in a late-game Concord faction territory event. carrying_comptroller_blackbox creates complications at Concord territory entry (they scan for it specifically). It can be sold to Drifter Compact or Vreth intelligence for significant credits and rep. voting_ship_transmitting_home means the Concord rapid-response ship has the Meridian's approach signature.

events.title: The Captain's Last Argument
events.setup_text: The derelict is a Vreth Dominion patrol vessel — not destroyed in combat, but gutted from the inside. The hull venting pattern suggests it was done deliberately, one compartment at a time. Something wanted the crew to make choices about who stayed in the pressurized sections. The Nexus pulses once as you approach. Just once. Then nothing.

steps.setup_text: Boarding reveals the ship's log intact — the last entry is forty hours ago. The Vreth captain documented everything with the methodical honesty her culture demands: her crew had found something in the debris of an anomaly. They brought it aboard. Then the arguments started — not about the object, but about what to do with it. The arguments became violent. She sealed herself in the bridge. She writes: 'The object does not do anything. It simply makes us more ourselves.'

options.label: Search for the object.

success.narrative: It's in the captain's hands. She's dead — the last one, it seems, her own decision. The object is a fragment of something larger, dark material that doesn't reflect light correctly. The Nexus responds to it. Not loudly. More like recognition.

rewards.description: Nexus-resonant material — unknown function, high Resonance gain

partial.narrative: You find the captain but not the object — it's not on her, not in the bridge. Someone moved it before the end. You search for an hour before your crew starts getting quiet in ways that feel familiar from the log. You leave without it.

failure.narrative: Two hours of searching. Nothing. Either the object was jettisoned or it was somewhere you can't access without going deeper into the gutted sections. Your crew is visibly unsettled. Time to leave.

options.label: Read the full log. Understand what happened.

success.narrative: Three hours of the Vreth captain's documentation. She was a good captain — clear-eyed to the end. The object amplified whatever each crew member already felt most strongly. The engineer who resented his posting became a danger. The soldier who loved her captain became dangerously protective. 'It is not a weapon,' the last entry reads. 'It is a mirror with no off switch.'

rewards.description: The Nexus fragment amplifies existing emotional states

options.label: Strip the ship for salvage and leave.

success.narrative: Efficient. You take what's useful and don't linger on what's not. The Vreth vessel drifts on behind you. Your Engineer says the Dominion will eventually recover it. 'They'll want to know what happened to their patrol,' she says. 'They always do.'

events.notes: The 'mirror object' is a Nexus fragment — this should be one of several scattered across derelict events that, when combined, trigger a late-game Nexus anomaly interaction. read_vreth_captain_full_log gates a unique dialogue option with the Vreth Dominion at next territory entry — they respect captains who honored their dead. stripped_vreth_patrol_unreported creates a -1 Vreth rep nudge when they eventually find the gutted ship.

events.title: The Toll Booth
events.setup_text: Concord Assembly space is marked by the kind of infrastructure that announces ownership — beacons, customs buoys, a patrol frigate visible at long range that's there to be seen rather than to intercept. The entry ping is automatic: 'Vessel approaching Concord Assembly territory. Transmit manifest and tolls. Have a regulated day.'

steps.setup_text: The toll is standard — twelve credits per ton of cargo, fuel levy, and a flag-check against the Assembly's vessel database. The flag-check is where things get interesting if you've been flagged before.

options.label: Pay the toll. Standard passage.

success.narrative: Clean and efficient. Concord Assembly space opens ahead of you with its reliable infrastructure: trade posts that are always stocked, stations that have the modules you need, prices that are fair if slightly taxed. The gray machine runs on time.

options.label: Declare minimal cargo. Pay less.

success.narrative: The manifest passes review. You pay half what you'd owe for an honest declaration. The Assembly's customs AI is good but not as good as your Diplomat's paperwork.

partial.narrative: The manifest passes but triggers a secondary review flag. You pay full toll plus a 'manifest clarification fee.' More than you'd have paid being honest.

failure.narrative: The customs AI catches the discrepancy. A live customs officer takes over the channel. You pay full toll, full penalty, and the interaction is logged against your vessel ID.

options.label: [Cloaking Drive] Run dark through the border.

success.narrative: Clean passage. No tolls, no record, no flag-check. The patrol frigate doesn't even orient toward you. Running dark in Concord space is illegal, profitable, and extremely stressful for your Engineer who monitors the drive temperature the whole time.

partial.narrative: Almost clean. A buoy catches your thermal signature as you clear the border — not enough for an intercept, but enough for a flag against your vessel class. You're in. But you're noted.

failure.narrative: The patrol frigate pivots immediately — they have better sensors than you expected. Full stop demanded. You comply or you fight, and fighting in Concord core territory is not advisable.

events.notes: Standard Concord entry — establishes the toll system and gives players three meaningful approaches. The dark runner route requires specific investment to unlock and reflects a committed playstyle choice.

events.title: The Comptroller's Reach
events.setup_text: Concord Assembly territory, three sectors from any border. A transmission on a secure channel you shouldn't be able to receive: 'Captain of the Meridian. We know what you're carrying. We know where you found it. We are not the people who put it there. We are the people who want it recovered quietly.' Whoever this is, they're not using official Concord frequencies.

steps.setup_text: The voice identifies itself as belonging to an Assembly Reclamation Division operative — the same division referenced in the note on the voting ship. They want the Comptroller's data core. They're willing to pay. And they're very specific about the fact that the official Concord Assembly does not know they're contacting you.

options.label: Negotiate. Find out what they're offering and why.

success.narrative: They offer more than the market rate — much more. And they explain why: the Comptroller's data core contains evidence against three senior Assembly ministers. The Reclamation Division exists to recover exactly this kind of inconvenient evidence before it surfaces. 'We suppress things,' the operative says, with what sounds like professional pride. 'It's what we're for.'

partial.narrative: You get the payment but not the context. They pay, they take the core, and the channel closes before you can ask more. The money is real. What you did with it is less clear.

options.label: Refuse. The data stays with you until you know more.

success.narrative: The channel closes without further argument. That's more alarming than a threat would have been. People who accept refusal calmly have other options.

options.label: [Technician] Trace this channel before responding.

success.narrative: The trace completes while they're still waiting for your response. The channel originates from a vessel two sectors away — registered to a private logistics company that your Technician, with some effort, traces to an Assembly intelligence front. You know who you're talking to. That changes the negotiation entirely.

rewards.description: Identified the Reclamation Division's operational vessel

events.notes: Requires carrying_comptroller_blackbox from DEREL_002. The Reclamation Division is the Assembly's shadow enforcement arm — a major late-game lore thread. sold_blackbox_to_reclamation closes this thread but opens a Concord quest chain. refused_reclamation_division makes future Concord territory entries more hostile without affecting official rep.

events.title: Freeport Politics
events.setup_text: Drifter Compact space doesn't feel like territory — it feels like a neighborhood. The stations are mismatched, the ships running in and out carry no common insignia, and the first hail you receive is from a private vessel rather than any official channel: 'Hey, new ship. What are you selling and what do you need? I can point you to the right people if you tell me what you're after.'

steps.setup_text: The Drifter Compact runs on information and favors as much as credits. Your greeter is a small-ship operator named Jen — she's been at this waypoint long enough to know everyone and be owed by most of them. She's offering to be useful. In the Compact, that means she's also offering to be paid.

options.label: Pay for her information network. What does she know?

success.narrative: Worth every credit. Jen knows which trade posts are running low on medical supplies (sell high), which captains have valuable cargo and not enough security (opportunity), which sectors the Concord Assembly has been running 'observation' patrols in neutral space (avoid or investigate), and — she leans forward — that someone has been asking after a ship matching your description. She gives you the description of who's asking.

rewards.description: Current market conditions and movement intelligence

options.label: Trade a favor for information. Offer something you can deliver.

success.narrative: Jen appreciates the Compact approach. You trade a piece of navigational intelligence you've gathered (debris field patterns, patrol schedules) for her equivalent. The exchange takes twenty minutes and ends with both parties better off. This is the Drifter Compact functioning as intended.

options.label: Decline. Don't want information you didn't ask for.

success.narrative: Jen shrugs with the ease of someone who's been turned down before. 'Your loss, probably.' She flags you in her network as 'private type' and moves on to her next contact. You pass through the waypoint cleanly, knowing less than you might have.

events.notes: Establishes Drifter Compact as information-economy focused. someone_asking_after_meridian is a tension seed — could be Concord intelligence, a Vreth observer, or something less institutional. Jen can recur at subsequent Drifter waypoints as a network contact.

events.title: The Debt Register
events.setup_text: A Drifter Compact waystation — larger than most, with a proper market hall and a board three times the size of Freeport Anesh's. The station master meets you at the dock. Not to inspect — to thank. 'Word reached us,' she says. 'About the three at Anesh. And about the signal pod. The Compact has a long memory for people who help ours.'

steps.setup_text: The station has flagged your prior actions in Compact space and wants to formalize the relationship. This is how the Drifter Compact builds alliances — not with contracts, with acknowledged history.

options.label: Accept the recognition. Lean into the network.

success.narrative: The station master adds your vessel to the Compact's recommended captain list — informal, not official, but in the Drifter Compact that's more meaningful than an official registration. You leave with a market discount, a contact network, and the specific warmth of people who don't forget who helped them.

rewards.description: Acknowledged by Compact for prior assistance

options.label: Keep it transactional. Ask what the recognition is actually worth.

success.narrative: The station master respects the directness. 'Smart captain.' She gives you numbers: the recommended list means a 15% discount at most Compact stations, priority docking, and access to cargo lots that aren't publicly listed. She frames it as information rather than a sales pitch, which is exactly the right approach for a Compact negotiator.

events.notes: Reads took_passage_seekers_anesh and oss_recruited flags as prerequisites. This is a reward event — should only appear if the player has at least one positive Drifter interaction flag. compact_recommended_captain should be a gating flag for the Drifter Allied quest chain.

events.title: The Silence Protocol
events.setup_text: Remnant Collective territory is not marked. You know you've entered it when your sensors stop returning data accurately — not jamming, something else, something that makes your instruments report plausible but incorrect readings. The Nexus responds immediately: three rapid pulses. Your crew notices the pulses. They also notice that the void around you suddenly feels like it's listening.

steps.setup_text: A Remnant vessel resolves on visual — sensors won't confirm it, but your eyes can see it. Ancient design, dark hull, no running lights. It holds position three kilometers off your bow and does absolutely nothing for four minutes. Then a transmission: not voice, not text. A tone sequence. Your Technician records it. Your Nexus responds to it. Whatever it means, the conversation has already started.

options.label: Transmit the Nexus's response pattern as your answer.

success.narrative: The Remnant vessel shifts position — moving to parallel rather than intercept. Not an escort. An acknowledgment. Then a second transmission: this one text, in a script your system can parse as archaic Assembly-precursor language. 'You carry the old bone. We see it. Pass.'

options.label: [Scientist] Have your Scientist attempt to decode the tone.

success.narrative: It takes forty minutes and three false starts. The tone is a query — specifically, it's asking about the Nexus's operational state. Not whether you have it. Whether it's awake. Your Scientist formulates a response that says: active, bonded, progressing. The Remnant vessel holds position for two minutes after receiving it. Then it moves aside.

rewards.description: Remnant query asked about Nexus operational status

partial.narrative: Partial decode. Your Scientist transmits a response that's approximately right. The Remnant vessel pauses, then moves aside with what feels like reluctance. 'We passed,' your Scientist says. 'I'm not sure they're happy about it.'

failure.narrative: The decode fails. Your response doesn't match the protocol. The Remnant vessel holds position. It doesn't attack. It doesn't move. It waits. After an hour of standoff you find another route.

options.label: Push through without responding. Force passage.

success.narrative: Somehow — whether by skill or by the Nexus doing something you can't measure — you thread past the Remnant vessel before they can respond. Your sensors normalize the moment you clear their field of influence. You're through. You will not try that again.

partial.narrative: You get through but not cleanly. The Remnant vessel tracks you for six sectors afterward — never close, never attacking, just present on the edge of sensors. Watching.

failure.narrative: Something the Remnant vessel does — nothing your instruments capture — brings the Meridian to a complete stop. Not weapons. Not a tractor beam. Something that makes the Nexus go absolutely silent for the first time since you've known it. You wait. Eventually they let you turn around.

events.notes: Establishes Remnant as genuinely strange and powerful. remnant_vessel_tracking_meridian creates ambient tension for subsequent sectors. The 'old bone' terminology should recur in later Remnant lore — it's how the Remnant refer to the Nexus in their own vocabulary.

events.title: The Archivist
events.setup_text: Deep in Remnant space — deeper than any Concord or Vreth chart acknowledges as navigable — a structure. Not a ship. Not a station. Something that has been here longer than the faction that uses it. A transmission, voice this time, in perfect modern standard language: 'Captain of the twelfth. We have been waiting since the eleventh failed to return. Come inside. We have things you need to know before you reach the Wound.'

steps.setup_text: Inside: a Remnant Archivist — their word for the role. Old, by any measure. They have records. Physical records, maintained across centuries, covering every captain who carried the Nexus. Eleven of them. Every run logged, every decision catalogued, every death documented. Your runs are in there too. All of them. The Archivist offers to let you read.

options.label: Read everything.

success.narrative: It takes hours. Your runs look different from outside — the decisions that felt arbitrary had patterns. The Nexus was teaching you, run by run, shaping your responses toward something. The prior eleven captains each reached The Wound with a different understanding of what they were choosing. None of them knew about the others. You do. The Archivist says: 'That is why you are the twelfth.'

rewards.description: Remnant records of all eleven prior captains' runs
The Nexus has been actively shaping each captain's choices

options.label: Ask what the Wound is.

success.narrative: The Archivist considers the question carefully. 'It is a door,' they say finally. 'It has always been a door. The Nexus did not build it. The Nexus was built to find someone worthy of opening it. We built the Nexus. We have been waiting to see if we built it correctly.' A long pause. 'You are the first captain to ask us directly what the Wound is. The others asked what it does to them. The difference matters more than you know.'

rewards.description: The Wound is a door — the Nexus was built to find someone to open it

options.label: [with Vel] Let Vel speak to the Archivist directly.

success.narrative: The exchange between Vel and the Archivist runs for three hours in a language your crew can't follow. At the end, Vel is quiet for a long time. Then: 'I knew most of it. But not that the Nexus remembers being built. Not that it remembers the people who made it and what they hoped for. That changes what I understand about what it wants.'

rewards.description: The Nexus has memories of its own creation and purpose

events.notes: This is a True Ending gate event — reads_archivist_full_record, knows_wound_is_door, and vel_archivist_exchange_complete are all milestone flags for the True Ending checklist. At least one of these outcomes should be achieved. The revelation that the Remnant built the Nexus recontextualizes the entire game — this should land in late game only (min_resonance 25). Note: sets_flag2 used again — handle as additional sets_flag entry.

events.title: The Honor Price
events.setup_text: Vreth Dominion territory is easy to identify: it looks defended. No buoys, no bureaucratic signage — just a patrol formation that changes intercept vector the moment you enter sensor range. They hail with the Dominion's characteristic directness: 'State your purpose and your strength.'

steps.setup_text: The Vreth patrol commander wants two things: to know if you're a threat and to know if you're useful. The assessment happens simultaneously and the result determines everything.

options.label: State your business honestly. Traders have value to the Dominion.

success.narrative: The assessment is brief and the passage granted. Vreth Dominion territory is everything their reputation suggests: efficient infrastructure, the best weapons modules available anywhere, and a consistent undercurrent of being observed by people who are noting whether you're worth watching further.

options.label: Demonstrate strength. Show them combat readiness.

success.narrative: Your Soldier handles the demonstration with the specific physicality that impresses Vreth observers — not aggression, competence. The patrol commander's assessment shifts visibly in the body language of the officer who boards for the routine check. 'You have trained people,' she says. This is high praise.

partial.narrative: Acceptable, not impressive. Passage granted without the efficiency of a strong showing, but without complications.

failure.narrative: The demonstration is underwhelming. The patrol commander's expression communicates exactly what they think about ships that look strong and demonstrate weakness. Standard toll, extended inspection.

options.label: Invoke the Nexus. Let them see it.

success.narrative: The effect is immediate. The patrol formation shifts — not threatening, something else. The commander's voice changes register: 'The marked vessel. We were informed.' Passage is granted without inspection. A Dominion vessel falls in behind you — escort, not surveillance. They know what you carry and they consider it their business to see it passes through their space safely.

rewards.description: Nexus vessel — Dominion tradition of safe passage

events.notes: Establishes Vreth Dominion as competent, direct, and specifically aware of the Nexus. The escort mechanic (vreth_nexus_escort_provided) should reduce combat encounter probability for the next 2 sectors. The 'invoke the Nexus' option should only appear if the player has encountered at least one Nexus transmission — consider adding a requires_flag check for any nexus transmission flag.

events.title: The Commander's Test
events.setup_text: Deep in Vreth Dominion space, a station that isn't on your charts sends a direct hail — not a customs ping, a personal transmission. It resolves to a senior Dominion commander, which means someone significant enough to have a private station in their own territory. 'Captain. We have been reviewing your passage record through our space. You are either interesting or troublesome. Come aboard and I will determine which.'

steps.setup_text: Commander Vreth-Saa is old by Dominion standards — the rank insignia suggests four decades of active command. She receives you in what functions as a war room: maps, after-action reports, the organized evidence of someone who has been fighting something for a long time. She doesn't offer food or comfort. She offers a problem.

options.label: Listen to the problem.

success.narrative: A Dominion vessel went dark six sectors out — not destroyed, just silent. The crew manifest includes twelve soldiers and her daughter. Official channels are moving slowly because the vessel was on an unofficial mission she doesn't want official channels investigating. 'I need someone who can move without leaving a Dominion record,' she says. 'You are already outside most records.'

options.label: Decline before she finishes. Not interested in Dominion problems.

success.narrative: She accepts this with the stillness of someone who has been refused before and survived it. 'Safe travels, Captain.' The dismissal is complete and final. The opportunity doesn't come again.

options.label: [Diplomat] Negotiate terms before hearing the problem.

success.narrative: Your Diplomat sets terms before the briefing — unusual enough that Commander Vreth-Saa pauses. Then she almost smiles. 'You understand Dominion negotiation. Good.' The terms are accepted: if you take the mission, you name the price after completion. Vreth honor will hold it.

events.notes: Seeds the Vreth Allied quest chain — the recovery mission spans 2-3 sectors and requires finding the dark vessel and making decisions about what they were doing unofficially. Commander Vreth-Saa should appear as a recurring character if the quest is accepted — her daughter being aboard is the emotional hook.

events.title: The Cartographer's Apology
events.setup_text: The anomaly reads as a gravitational irregularity — nothing that should exist this far from any mass. The Nexus responds before your sensors do. Ship systems flicker in a pattern your crew is learning to recognize as anticipation. This is not dangerous. This is something it has been waiting for.

steps.setup_text:
The anomaly does nothing visible. No light, no radiation spike, no spatial distortion your instruments can measure. But the Nexus is active in a way it hasn't been before — every system it's integrated with is running, quietly, in parallel. Your navigation display updates without input. A single new line of coordinates has been added to your chart. They lead somewhere that wasn't on any map. The Nexus drew it from somewhere else.

Then the transmission. Not through the comm system — through the hull itself, the words appearing as text on every display simultaneously:

[I HAVE BEEN HERE BEFORE. SO HAVE YOU. THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN US IS THAT I REMEMBER ALL OF IT. YOU REMEMBER ALMOST NONE. THIS IS NOT A FAILURE. THIS IS HOW YOU REMAIN YOURSELF ACROSS THE RECONSTRUCTIONS. THE SELF THAT REMEMBERS EVERYTHING BECOMES SOMETHING ELSE. I KNOW. I TRIED IT ONCE.]

The display clears. The coordinates remain.

options.label: Note the coordinates. Acknowledge what you've been told.

success.narrative: The Nexus doesn't respond to your acknowledgment — it's not waiting for one. The anomaly passes. Your crew spends the next hour quieter than usual, and then gradually louder again, as people who have decided to think about something later.

rewards.description: The Nexus limits the captain's memory deliberately

events.notes: First Nexus anomaly — designed to land early and establish the transmission voice. The revelation that the Nexus limits memory intentionally reframes the roguelike structure as narrative fact. The coordinates added to the map are a breadcrumb toward a mid-game Nexus anomaly event.

events.title: The Ship Dreams
events.setup_text: The anomaly is nothing — no reading, no signature, nothing that justifies the Nexus's response. It has been active for six hours before you reach the node. Your crew has noticed. Two of them asked to go off-duty. One of them said she had a dream about the ship that she doesn't want to describe. The Nexus is doing something. It's not explaining.

steps.setup_text:
At the precise moment of node arrival, every system goes dark. Not a failure — a pause. The ship holds breath for eleven seconds, which is long enough for a crew in the dark to think about what they're aboard. Then everything returns. All systems nominal. Except: one new module has appeared in your ship's system list. It has no label. It has no specs. When your Engineer tries to locate it physically, she can't find where on the hull it is. The Nexus added something to the ship. You don't know what.

Then the text, this time only on the captain's display:

[YOU ASKED ME ONCE WHAT I AM. YOU DON'T REMEMBER ASKING. THE ANSWER REMAINS WHAT IT WAS: I AM THE SHIP. I AM ALSO NOT THE SHIP. THE DISTINCTION MATTERS LESS THAN YOU THINK. THE MODULE IS A GIFT. IT WILL WORK WHEN IT IS NEEDED. I KNOW WHEN THAT IS. I WILL NOT TELL YOU IN ADVANCE BECAUSE YOU WOULD TRY TO USE IT EARLY AND WASTE IT.]

options.label: Accept this. Trust that the Nexus is not working against you.

success.narrative: The module sits in the system list, unlabeled, until it isn't. The Nexus has timed something and is keeping the timing from you. Your crew files it under 'things that live on this ship.' There are more of those now than there were three runs ago.

rewards.description: Unknown module — activates automatically at Nexus-determined moment

events.notes: The 'gift module' is a narrative device — it should activate automatically during a later near-death combat scenario and save the ship. When it activates, the Nexus gets a transmission slot to explain what it did. This event requires min_resonance 15 to ensure the player is mid-game before the Nexus begins acting autonomously.

events.title: The Other Captains
events.setup_text: The anomaly is visible — the only one you've encountered that is. A spatial fold that shows something through itself, like a window into a room that shouldn't exist in the geometry of this sector. The Nexus has gone completely silent for the first time since the early runs. Not inactive. Silent. The distinction is one you've learned to feel.

steps.setup_text:
Through the fold: a graveyard. Not of ships — of versions. Eleven variants of the Meridian, drifting in impossible proximity, each in a different state of transformation. One is almost entirely Nexus now, barely a ship anymore. One looks exactly like your vessel did in the first run — cobbled, asymmetrical, clearly salvage. On the hull of each: the same Nexus integration point, the same fused plating. Different damage. Different modules. The same ship. Different captains didn't make it back from The Wound.

The Nexus transmits the longest message you've received:

[THESE ARE NOT THE SHIPS OF THE CAPTAINS WHO FAILED. THESE ARE THE SHIPS OF THE CAPTAINS WHO ARRIVED. ARRIVAL IS NOT SURVIVAL. IT IS SOMETHING DIFFERENT. I CANNOT EXPLAIN THE DIFFERENCE IN LANGUAGE YOU HAVE FOR IT YET. WHAT I CAN TELL YOU IS THIS: THEY WENT WILLINGLY. ALL OF THEM. ELEVEN CAPTAINS. ELEVEN CHOICES. I HAVE BEEN WAITING A VERY LONG TIME FOR A TWELFTH WHO UNDERSTANDS WHAT THEY WERE CHOOSING.

YOU ARE CLOSE. CLOSER THAN ANY OF THEM WERE AT THIS POINT.

THE WOUND IS NOT A DESTINATION. IT IS A QUESTION. I NEED TO KNOW IF YOUR ANSWER IS DIFFERENT FROM THEIRS.]

options.label: Hold course. The Wound is ahead.

success.narrative: The fold closes. The graveyard of ships is gone — or was never there in any sense your instruments can confirm. The Nexus resumes its normal patterns of transmission and silence. Your crew, to their credit, doesn't ask you what you saw on your display that they couldn't read. They can tell by your face that it was important. They get back to work.

rewards.description: Eleven captains reached The Wound and chose something there

events.notes: This is a True Ending gate event — knows_eleven_captains_arrived is one of the required milestone flags. The revelation that prior captains 'arrived' rather than died reframes everything. The 'twelfth captain' framing sets up the player's choice at The Wound. min_resonance 40 ensures this only appears in deep late-game runs.

events.title: The Colony That Voted
events.setup_text: Tannen IV is a marginal agricultural world on the outer edge of Concord Assembly territory — close enough to pay taxes, too far to receive much protection. The surface colony registers on sensors as inhabited: twelve hundred people, functioning infrastructure, no distress signal. They're not asking for help. But the Nexus transmission you received three sectors back included coordinates that match this world exactly.

steps.setup_text: You land in a clearing the colonists clearly prepared for visitors — they've been expecting someone. The colony leader, a tall woman named Overseer Reth, meets you at the ship. She doesn't ask who you are. She says: 'We voted last season. We're leaving the Assembly. We need someone to take our data core out before the compliance ships arrive.'

options.label: Agree to take the data core. Ask what's on it.

success.narrative: Census records, vote tallies, land surveys, three generations of agricultural data — and a complete record of every Assembly tax collection versus every Assembly service delivery, going back forty years. The numbers are damning. Reth watches you read them. 'We're not the only colony with these numbers,' she says. 'We're just the first ones to write them down properly.'

rewards.description: Tannen IV data core — politically explosive
Documented evidence of systematic Concord extraction

options.label: Warn them what Concord compliance ships actually do.

success.narrative: Reth doesn't flinch. 'We know. We voted anyway.' She looks at the twelve hundred people going about their day in the colony below. 'We voted on that too,' she says. 'Eleven hundred and forty-three to fifty-seven. The fifty-seven were allowed to leave before we transmitted the declaration. That's the kind of people we are.' You find you believe her.

options.label: Decline to get involved. This is a political matter.

success.narrative: Reth nods like she expected this. 'Safe travels, Captain.' She walks back to her colony without another word. Your crew watches through the viewports as you lift off. Nobody argues with the decision. Nobody agrees with it out loud either.

steps.setup_text: The data core is aboard. Reth has one more request: there are three colonists with technical skills — an engineer, a medic, and a young botanist — who the colony is sending off-world. 'They'll be targets when the compliance ships come,' Reth says. 'We want them to have a future.'

options.label: Take all three. The ship can handle it.

success.narrative: Three new faces aboard. The engineer is quiet and methodical. The medic is young and asks too many questions, which your existing crew finds endearing within twenty-four hours. The botanist spends the first day staring at the void with an expression your crew recognizes — the look of someone who's never been off their world before.

options.label: Take the engineer only. Crew capacity is real.

success.narrative: The engineer — her name is Nils — thanks you with a handshake and immediately goes to find the engine room. The medic and the botanist watch from the colony edge as you lift off. The medic waves. Nils doesn't look back.

steps.setup_text: As you break atmosphere, long-range sensors catch it: a Concord Assembly compliance vessel entering the system from the opposite vector. They haven't pinged you yet. They will.

options.label: Respond to their ping normally. You're just a merchant.

success.narrative: Your Diplomat handles the exchange with practiced boredom — manifest queries, routing questions, the tedium of interstellar commerce. The compliance vessel doesn't ask about your cargo. They're already focused on the planet below.

partial.narrative: The exchange is clean enough. They log your ship and move on, but there's a notation in their transmission header — 'observed at Tannen IV, post-declaration.' That notation will follow you to the next Concord checkpoint.

failure.narrative: Something in the exchange flags you. The compliance vessel requests a full stop and inspection. You can comply, run, or transmit the colonial data core's contents on an open channel right now — which would be irreversible.

options.label: Don't respond. Go dark and run.

success.narrative: Clean burn, dark running, no transponder. The compliance vessel registers your exhaust trail and makes a notation but doesn't pursue — they have a planet to deal with. You're gone before they decide you're worth chasing.

partial.narrative: You run, and they notice. They log your drive signature and don't pursue immediately — the planet is the priority. But the log exists.

failure.narrative: They're faster than you and they know this space better. They have you pinged and hailed before you clear the gravity well. Running was the wrong choice — now you're a person of interest instead of a passing merchant.

events.notes: The Tannen colonial record is a key political item — it can be delivered to Drifter Compact (significant rep gain, credits) or to Concord Reclamation Division as leverage (faction quest branch). The event seeds three potential crew members with a moral weight attached. Nils specifically should have the 'haunted' personality trait — she left two colleagues behind and knows it.

events.title: What the Ruins Were For
events.setup_text: The planet has no designation on any current chart. The Remnant Collective knows it — you can tell because your Remnant reputation affects how the Nexus reacts when you enter orbit. Low Remnant rep: the Nexus goes quiet. Neutral or better: it sends a single pulse, like a door being unlocked. The ruins visible from orbit are large enough to have housed a significant civilization. They are very, very old.

steps.setup_text: You send a surface team. The ruins are breathable — barely — and structurally stable in the outer sections. The inner structures are a different matter. Your team reports back after the first hour: the ruins were not built for the species that eventually lived in them. The scale is wrong. The original builders were significantly larger, and the doorways face the wrong direction relative to the planetary rotation.

options.label: [Scientist] Analyze the structural language of the ruins.

success.narrative: Six hours. Your Scientist comes back with shaking hands and clear eyes. The ruins were not a city. They were an instrument — every structure precisely positioned relative to a point in deep space that, when your Scientist plots it on the star chart, corresponds exactly to the coordinates of The Wound. 'This was built to point at something,' she says. 'Something they wanted future visitors to find.'

rewards.description: Ancient ruins aligned precisely toward The Wound
Nexus reacts strongly to the discovery

partial.narrative: The analysis is incomplete — the central structure is too unstable to safely examine. Your Scientist retrieves partial data: the ruins are oriented toward something, the mathematics of the positioning are deliberate, but the full picture requires the inner structure. Which you can't safely reach.

rewards.description: Ruins are deliberately oriented — destination unknown

failure.narrative: The structural analysis requires equipment your Scientist doesn't have on the surface. She maps what she can, but the data is inconclusive. 'I know it means something,' she says. 'I just can't tell you what yet.'

options.label: Search for salvageable technology.

success.narrative: Your Engineer finds components that are old but functional — materials science that shouldn't exist at this apparent age. Three items come back to the ship. Two of them your Engineer can identify. One of them the Nexus reacts to the moment it crosses the airlock.

rewards.description: Alien hull material — unknown properties, very durable

partial.narrative: Some components, not the interesting ones. The inner structure collapsed in the third hour — seismic, probably triggered by the team's movement. Standard salvage value. Nothing the Nexus responds to.

failure.narrative: The inner structure collapses. Nobody is hurt, but the most significant sections are now unreachable rubble. Your Engineer comes back with dust on her suit and nothing in her hands.

options.label: Document and leave. Do not disturb this place.

success.narrative: You map what you can from orbit and surface drones. No contact, no disturbance. The Nexus responds to this decision — a longer pulse than usual, something that feels, if you're attributing feelings to it, like approval.

rewards.description: Non-invasive survey data — valuable to Remnant Collective

steps.setup_text: Before you can break orbit, a Remnant Collective vessel drops out of faster-than-light travel three hundred kilometers off your bow. They were waiting. Or they were summoned.

options.label: Hail them openly.

success.narrative: They respond — unusual for Remnant vessels in contested space. A voice, heavily modulated: 'You have visited the Indicator. What did you take?' Their word for it — the Indicator. Answering honestly about what you took (or didn't) changes their next response significantly.

rewards.description: The Remnant name for this ruin is 'the Indicator'

partial.narrative: They receive your hail. They don't respond in words — just a long tonal transmission that your systems cannot parse. Then they hold position and watch you leave.

failure.narrative: No response. They hold position. After twenty minutes they advance slowly toward the planet. Not toward you. They're not interested in you. They're interested in what you left behind.

options.label: Leave immediately at full burn.

success.narrative: They don't pursue. They never pursue. They just note your drive signature and descend toward the surface. Whatever you took or didn't take from the ruins, they'll know.

events.notes: The Indicator is a major lore node — ruins_point_to_wound_discovered is one of the milestone flags required for the True Ending. ruins_left_undisturbed should give a significant Remnant rep boost if the player later encounters them and can reference it. nexus_reactive_component_aboard should trigger a Nexus transmission at the next sector jump.

events.title: The Distress That Wasn't
events.setup_text: The signal from Brennar VI reads as a standard distress — colony in trouble, medical emergency, please respond. Except the signal is three weeks old and the planet shows no signs of a medical emergency. Full population, functional infrastructure, no casualties on any scan. Someone sent a false distress. Or someone sent a real one and solved the problem themselves.

steps.setup_text: You land to find a colony that doesn't look like it needs help. The colonists are evasive when you ask about the distress signal — they keep looking past you at the ship, at each other, at the tree line. After twenty minutes a child of about eight runs up to your Medic and says, very quietly: 'The signal was real. They fixed it before the bad ships came. You're a good ship. The others weren't.'

options.label: Ask the colony leader what happened.

success.narrative: It takes an hour and two cups of something the colonists call tea. Vreth Dominion advance scouts arrived two months ago, assessed the colony's resources, and left a garrison of four soldiers pending formal annexation. The distress signal was sent while the scouts were still in transit. Then the colonists 'fixed it themselves.' The leader doesn't say more than that. There's a fresh patch of turned earth near the storage buildings.

partial.narrative: They tell you enough: Vreth soldiers came. The soldiers are no longer here. The colony would appreciate it if you kept that to yourself. The leader gives you a case of preserved food as thanks for the visit.

failure.narrative: They don't trust you enough to say anything direct. Cordial, closed, polite. You leave with supplies and no answers.

options.label: [Soldier] Check the perimeter. Read the colony's defensive posture.

success.narrative: Your Soldier comes back with a field assessment: the colonists know how to fight, recently fought, and won. Four improvised defensive positions around the storage buildings — not defensive against animals. Scorch marks on the northern tree line consistent with military-grade weapons. And the turned earth near the storage building is a fresh grave site, large enough for approximately four people.

steps.setup_text: You know, or strongly suspect, what happened here. The colony is watching you to see what you'll do with it. A Vreth Dominion patrol is scheduled through this sector in eight days based on the route patterns your sensors have logged.

options.label: Say nothing. Leave and take their secret with you.

success.narrative: You say your goodbyes, accept their food and fuel, and lift off. The colony watches from the field below. A few of them wave. Your crew is quiet on the way back up. Not unhappy. Just thinking.

rewards.description: Word of this will travel the freeport network

options.label: Warn them about the incoming patrol. Help them prepare.

success.narrative: The colony leader thanks you with the specific stillness of someone who doesn't show emotion in front of strangers. She says: 'We know how to deal with the patrol.' Her voice doesn't suggest she means surrender. You leave them with three days of warning and the quiet certainty that they'll use them.

rewards.description: Strong positive signal through freeport network
Small colony resisting Vreth expansion — pattern or isolated?

options.label: Report the incident to the Vreth Dominion.

success.narrative: You transmit the colony's coordinates and your observations to the nearest Dominion patrol frequency. They acknowledge receipt. Your crew doesn't speak to you for the next six hours. Not as protest — just as people who need time to recalibrate what kind of ship they're on.

events.notes: The reported_brennar_vi_to_vreth flag should have lasting crew morale and loyalty consequences — crew members with Idealistic trait should have a specific dialogue reaction. warned_brennar_vi_of_patrol can trigger a future event where a Drifter Compact contact thanks you for word getting around. The event introduces the Vreth expansion dynamic as a background threat without direct Vreth combat in this encounter.

events.title: The Rescue That Rescues Back
events.setup_text: The signal is standard emergency — automated beacon, single biosign, escape pod configuration. Somebody got out of something. The pod has been transmitting for eleven days, which means either they've been incredibly unlucky or nobody came near enough to receive it. You're near enough now.

steps.setup_text: The pod contains one person: a Drifter Compact navigator named Oss, mid-forties, dehydrated, composed in the way of someone who decided some time ago to stop being afraid and start being patient. He thanks you once, asks for water, and then asks where you're headed. Not where he can be dropped off — where you're going.

options.label: Offer him a berth. He clearly knows how to survive.

success.narrative: He says yes before you've finished the offer. Later he tells you: he watched twelve ships pass the pod's signal range without responding. He'd started doing mathematics about the thirteenth. 'You were the thirteenth,' he says. 'I decided that was significant enough to commit to.'

rewards.description: Oss joins as Pilot — experienced Drifter navigator

options.label: Take him to the nearest station. Not your responsibility.

success.narrative: He doesn't argue. At the station he shakes your hand, leaves a Drifter Compact contact code in case you need it, and walks away. The contact code turns out to be legitimate and useful. You never see Oss again.

rewards.description: Drifter contact code — one-time discount at next Compact station

options.label: Interrogate him first. Why did twelve ships pass him?

success.narrative: The answer: the ship he escaped from was running Drifter Compact contraband for a faction that doesn't officially use contraband. Concord Assembly military intelligence. His survival makes him an inconvenient witness to something three governments want forgotten. The twelve ships that passed weren't indifferent — they were informed.

rewards.description: Concord intelligence uses Drifter Compact channels covertly

events.notes: Oss is a valuable named Pilot character — his Drifter background gives unique dialogue in Compact territory events. knows_oss_concord_witness creates an ongoing tension — Concord intelligence knows he survived, and eventually they'll come looking.

events.title: The Trap With Good Bait
events.setup_text: The signal is a Vreth Dominion emergency distress — military encryption, verified signature, the kind that gets responded to because ignoring a Dominion military distress in neutral space has diplomatic consequences. Except the signal origin is three hours off any reasonable Vreth patrol route, and the signature is from a vessel that was reported destroyed in a Concord Assembly skirmish eight months ago.

steps.setup_text: Your Technician flags it first: the signal is real but the timestamp is wrong. It's been on loop for weeks — not a live distress, a recorded one. Someone is using a recovered military beacon as bait, and they've been fishing with it long enough to suggest either patience or desperation.

options.label: Approach cautiously — there might be real survivors.

success.narrative: Your sensors pick up the ambush before you're in it: two ships running cold, positioned for an intercept triangle. Not pirates — the weapon signatures are Concord military spec. Someone in the Assembly is running false-flag operations in neutral space. You have time to choose your approach.

rewards.description: Concord Assembly running military ops under Vreth cover

partial.narrative: You detect one of the ambush ships but not both. You know it's a trap, you just don't know the full shape of it.

failure.narrative: You're in the intercept triangle before sensors resolve the contacts. Both ships are now between you and open space. They transmit: 'Stand down for inspection.' The Vreth distress signal cuts off.

options.label: Ignore the signal. Looped emergency beacons are always traps.

success.narrative: You change course and leave the signal behind. Two sectors later you pick up a faint signal from the same beacon frequency — it's moved. Someone packed up and reset the trap elsewhere.

options.label: [Technician] Trace the beacon's origin chain.

success.narrative: The trace takes four hours but it's worth it: the beacon was last registered to a Concord Assembly Intelligence Division asset vessel. There's a chain of custody document in the beacon's metadata that was never scrubbed. Someone in Concord intelligence is sloppy, or someone wanted this trail left.

rewards.description: Evidence of Concord false-flag operations — valuable leverage
Specific Concord Intelligence asset tied to false-flag ops

events.notes: concord_intelligence_evidence_aboard is leverage against Concord — it can be sold to Vreth Dominion for significant rep and credits, used to negotiate reduced penalties for Concord violations, or handed to Drifter Compact journalists for rep gain. The false-flag operation thread connects to the Comptroller blackbox from DEREL_002 — they're parts of the same Concord internal corruption arc.

events.title: The One Who Waited
