events.setup_text: The signal has no format your systems recognize. It's not an emergency beacon, not a faction frequency, not a navigational marker. Your Technician spends two hours on it before saying: 'It's not broadcasting. It's responding. To something on this ship.' The Nexus is what it's responding to. Something out there has been waiting to detect a Nexus-bearing vessel, and now that it has, it's sending a very specific kind of hello.

steps.setup_text: The signal source is a small craft — not quite a ship, more like a survival platform that was built to last a very long time. One biosign, Remnant physiotype, active. They've been out here for a duration your crew argues about quietly: the platform's construction matches Remnant tech from three hundred years ago. Either they've been maintained by resupply — or the occupant is three hundred years old.

options.label: Bring them aboard.

success.narrative: The Remnant individual — who will eventually offer the name Vel, though they explain this is a translation, not a name — is physically similar enough to human to coexist. They speak slowly, choosing words with the precision of someone working in a third language. Their first question, after basic orientation: 'How many times have you died? The Nexus should know. It does not always share.'

rewards.description: Vel joins as Scientist — ancient Remnant, unique knowledge

options.label: Communicate remotely. Don't bring a Remnant individual aboard.

success.narrative: They accept this. Through the communication channel they transmit three things: a star chart correction that places The Wound more precisely than any faction's charts, a warning about a specific kind of Nexus anomaly to not enter unprepared, and a single question they say they have been waiting three hundred years to ask someone who might know: 'Does it still sing, at the deep nodes?' You don't know what this means. The Nexus does — it pulses three times in quick succession.

rewards.description: The Wound's exact coordinates from Remnant source
Nexus exhibits unusual behavior at specific deep anomalies

options.label: [Scientist] Let your Scientist lead the contact.

success.narrative: Your Scientist and Vel find a shared technical language within an hour — xenobiology and materials science crossing three hundred years of advancement in both directions. Vel boards willingly, drawn less by the Nexus and more by what your Scientist knows that they don't. The knowledge flows both ways.

rewards.description: Vel joins — deep Nexus knowledge, ancient tech expertise
Vel knows what the Nexus was originally built for

events.notes: Vel is a major named character — the only Remnant crew member available. Their ancient knowledge unlocks unique dialogue at Nexus anomalies and a special branch at The Wound itself. vel_recruited is a milestone flag. knows_nexus_original_purpose_hint is a True Ending gate flag — Vel's knowledge is required for the full resolution. Note: sets_flag2 is not standard schema — handle as additional sets_flag entry.

events.title: The Familiar Face
events.setup_text: The docking bay of Relay Station Yura smells like recycled air and old solder. You're halfway through a fuel transaction when the merchant — a broad-shouldered woman with Concord Assembly insignia tattooed along her collarbone — stops mid-sentence. Her eyes fix on the Meridian's hull. Specifically on the place where the Nexus has fused with the plating. She knows what she's looking at. You can see it in her face.

steps.setup_text: She doesn't run. She doesn't reach for anything. She just looks at you with an expression that sits somewhere between reverence and carefully managed fear. 'I've seen one of those before,' she says quietly. 'On a ship that came through seven years ago. The captain had the same look you do. Like they're not sure if they're being followed or led.'

options.label: Ask what happened to that captain.

success.narrative: She takes a long breath. 'Went deep,' she says. 'Past the Meridian Line, past where the charts stop. We got one transmission back — just coordinates and a single word I didn't recognize. Assembly researchers said it wasn't any language in the database.' She slides the fuel receipt across the counter. 'I don't know if they made it. I don't think anyone knows.' She doesn't charge you full price for the fuel.

rewards.description: Partial fuel discount from a spooked merchant

options.label: Deny it. Tell her it's just salvage plating.

success.narrative: She almost believes you. Not quite — she's seen one before, after all — but she lets it go. Finishes the transaction without another word. As you're leaving she says, almost too quiet to hear: 'If that's what it is, you should get it removed.' She doesn't look up.

partial.narrative: She doesn't believe you, but she appreciates the attempt at discretion. She finishes the transaction quickly, eyes darting to the dock entrance. 'Concord has people who watch for things like that,' she says. 'Just... move on quickly.'

failure.narrative: She sees straight through it and her expression closes like a door. She finishes the transaction in silence, but by the time you reach the dock exit two Assembly compliance officers are watching from the far end of the bay. They don't move. They just watch you leave.

options.label: [Diplomat] Ask if she wants to do business — quietly.

success.narrative: Your Diplomat steps forward with practiced ease, redirecting the conversation into something that feels less like a confession and more like a negotiation. An hour later you leave with a rare module pulled from beneath the counter — no manifest, no record — and a contact name scrawled on a fuel receipt. 'She buys things,' your Diplomat says. 'Things that don't officially exist.'

partial.narrative: The conversation goes well enough. She relaxes, sells you fuel at cost, and gives you a vague description of where the previous ship was headed. Not quite a contact. More of a warning dressed up as directions.

failure.narrative: Your Diplomat misjudges her — pushes too hard, too fast. She shuts down completely, completes the transaction by the numbers, and doesn't make eye contact again. The conversation that might have been is gone.

events.notes: Introductory event — designed to land early and establish that the Nexus is visible and known to some. The 'prior captain' rumor thread (flag: heard_prior_captain_rumor) should be picked up in a later Dead Zone ghost transmission. The Concord flag (concord_flagged_nexus_sighting_yura) can nudge Concord rep slightly at next territory entry.

events.title: The Tariff Collector
events.setup_text: Waypoint Kessler is Concord Assembly infrastructure at its most mundane: a gray ring station staffed by gray-uniformed clerks processing gray manifests. A compliance officer intercepts you at the docking queue — young, ambitious, clipboard in hand. 'Cargo declaration, please. We're running a sector-wide audit this cycle.'

steps.setup_text: The officer wants to inspect your hold. You have legitimate cargo, probably. The question is whether there's anything in the margins of your manifest that doesn't fully account for what's aboard.

options.label: Comply fully. Open the hold.

success.narrative: Clean and efficient. The officer finds nothing to flag, stamps your manifest with slightly more warmth than Concord officers usually manage, and waves you through. 'Safe travels, Captain.' You leave having paid standard docking fees and nothing else — and with a logged clean record that will matter at the next Assembly checkpoint.

options.label: Negotiate a flat fee to skip the inspection.

success.narrative: Your Diplomat frames it as 'administrative efficiency' rather than a bribe — a meaningful distinction in Concord territory. The officer accepts a flat fee, stamps the manifest unreviewed, and finds somewhere else to be. Everyone maintains plausible deniability.

partial.narrative: The officer won't skip the inspection, but your Diplomat does manage to limit it to surface cargo only. The hold's lower compartments go unchecked. Cost: standard fee plus a gratuity.

failure.narrative: The offer lands wrong — this officer is one of the ones who actually believes in the job. She flags the attempt, runs a full inspection out of principle, and charges you the maximum standard docking fee. 'I'll be noting this interaction,' she says.

options.label: [Technician] Loop the dock camera feed during inspection.

success.narrative: Thirty-two seconds of looped footage. The officer inspects nothing because she thinks she's inspecting everything. Your Technician is sweating by the time the feed restores, but there's no record of what she didn't find.

partial.narrative: The loop works but only covers part of the hold. The officer finds a minor manifest discrepancy — nothing major, fined and logged, not worth escalating. Your Technician considers it a learning experience.

failure.narrative: The feed glitch is visible. The officer notices the timestamp discontinuity immediately. What was a routine inspection becomes a thorough one, with two additional officers called in. Your Technician spends the next hour very quiet.

events.notes: Establishes Concord Assembly as bureaucratic but not toothless. concord_clean_inspection_logged should unlock a small discount at the next Concord trade post. concord_camera_tampering_flagged should nudge Concord rep -1 and add a customs alert at next territory entry.

events.title: Last Seat on the Lifeboat
events.setup_text: Freeport Anesh is a Drifter Compact station — no uniforms, no inspections, loud music from three different sources, and a board near the airlock where people post what they need and what they have. You're resupplying when you notice a cluster of people near the board. Not reading it — arguing about something posted there. A handwritten notice: PASSAGE NEEDED. WILL WORK. CANNOT PAY. THREE OF US.

steps.setup_text: The three people in question are easy to spot — they look like they've been on Anesh longer than they intended. A family unit, maybe, or something close to it: an older man with an engineer's calluses, a younger woman with the quiet watchfulness of someone who's spent time in faction military, and a teenager who keeps checking the station exits. They need out of this sector. The argument around the board is about whether anyone should take the liability.

options.label: Offer them passage in exchange for work.

success.narrative: The older man — Dorin — shakes your hand before you've finished the offer. Engineer's hands. The woman, Sera, takes longer; she watches your face for something she either finds or decides to trust. The teenager (no name offered yet) just follows. They're aboard within the hour. Dorin immediately starts cataloguing what's wrong with your propulsion system, which turns out to be quite a lot.

rewards.description: Dorin joins as Engineer — skilled specialist
Sera joins as Soldier — skilled specialist

options.label: Ask what they're running from first.

success.narrative: Sera answers before Dorin can stop her: Vreth Dominion territory dispute, a homestead that no longer exists, and a Dominion patrol that's been following them for six weeks. 'We're not wanted criminals,' she says. 'We're inconvenient witnesses.' Dorin looks at his hands. The teenager stares at the floor. Now you know, and knowing means deciding.

options.label: Walk past. Not your problem.

success.narrative: You resupply and leave. The three of them are still at the board when you disengage from the dock. Your crew doesn't say anything. Two of them watch the station out of the viewport until it falls behind the fog of war. Nobody brought it up. Nobody had to.

steps.setup_text: Now that you know — or now that you've agreed to take them — there's the question of the Vreth patrol. Sera says they're two sectors back. Dorin says one. The teenager says she counted the drive signatures and it's closer to eighteen hours.

options.label: Leave immediately. Burn extra fuel to open distance.

success.narrative: You're out of dock inside forty minutes. The fuel cost is real but the head start is real too. The teenager — her name is Maren, she eventually mentions — turns out to have an extraordinary intuition for drive signatures. She's been counting them her whole life.

options.label: [Pilot] Plot a route that loses any pursuit in the debris field.

success.narrative: Your Pilot threads the Meridian through the Ashfield's outer debris belt at a speed that makes the hull creak. When you emerge on the other side, there's nothing on sensors behind you. Dorin, watching from the engineering station, says: 'Good ship.' He means it.

partial.narrative: You lose them — probably. The debris field burns two extra fuel and leaves a fresh scar along the starboard hull, but sensors show nothing in pursuit when you clear the belt. Probably is good enough.

failure.narrative: The debris field is denser than charts indicated. You clip a tumbling hull section hard enough to compromise a sensor array and you come out the other side of the belt in roughly the direction you entered. Still ahead of pursuit — but not as far ahead.

rewards.description: Debris field incident logged by Concord buoy

events.notes: Dorin and Sera are named characters with unique dialogue — their recruitment flags should unlock specialist portraits. Maren (maren_drive_signature_talent_noted) is a seed for a later event where she can become a Pilot apprentice. abandoned_passage_seekers_anesh should echo in a later Drifter territory event — word gets around on freeports. knows_passage_seekers_vreth_history gates a Vreth-specific event variant where a patrol recognizes your passengers.
